participants.extracted: Adults aged 18–60 years with common mental disorders who were struggling with work participation were included. Participants were employees or working-age adults on sick leave, at risk of sick leave, or on long-term benefits (mainly work assessment allowance after more than 12 months of sick leave). Eligibility required clinician-assessed symptoms consistent with anxiety and/or depression, motivation to return to work or stay at work, and exclusion of severe psychiatric disorder, high suicide risk, pregnancy, ongoing substance abuse, inability to read Norwegian, psychotherapy elsewhere, or work problems primarily due to factors other than common mental disorders. A total of 1193 participants were randomized: 630 to AWaC and 563 to usual care. The sample was 67% women, mean age 40.4 years, and baseline work status included 31.4% working, 39% fully on sick leave, 21.7% on long-term benefits, and 7.9% unemployed.

interventions.extracted: The intervention was AWaC (At Work and Coping), an integrated programme combining work-focused cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT) with individual job support. It used miniteams of therapists and employment specialists to coordinate mental health treatment with an explicit work focus. The CBT component, described as ‘cognitive work-coping’, addressed mental health problems as they relate to work situations and offered up to 15 sessions. The job support component was based on the Individual Placement and Support (IPS) approach and was provided to participants who needed help with workplace adaptations or finding suitable employment, especially those on long-term benefits. IPS elements included consumer choice, focus on competitive employment, integration of mental health and employment services, attention to client preferences, work incentives planning, rapid job search, systematic job development, and individualized job support. The control group received usual care from general practitioners, the Norwegian Labour and Welfare Administration, and other health professionals, along with a letter encouraging use of available services and self-help resources; they were not restricted from receiving other employment or health services outside AWaC.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure work participation as the primary outcome at 12 months post baseline, operationalised as increased or maintained work participation, including maintained work participation, new employment, or full or partial return to work; this was assessed using national social insurance and employee register data over follow-up months up to 18 months. Secondary outcomes were measured at 6 and 12 months using self-report questionnaires and included psychological distress, anxiety, and depression symptoms assessed with the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS), and health-related quality of life assessed with EQ-5D. The study also planned to examine subgroup effects by baseline work status, gender, age, inclusion timing, and health-related characteristics, and to evaluate cost-effectiveness using registry and questionnaire data over the follow-up period.
